title: Accounts Payable & Payroll
subhead: The two largest cash outflows in any services firm — vendor invoices and payroll — and the controls that stop them from bleeding money through errors and fraud.
hook: "How do you process a vendor invoice?" Most candidates describe data entry. The right answer describes the three-way match, the hold-for-discrepancy process, and exactly which accounts move and when. This is the module that separates those two answers.

chunks.title: AP workflow and the 3-way match
chunks.bodyHtml:
Accounts Payable is not typing numbers into a system. It is a governed cycle designed to ensure the company only pays for goods and services it actually authorized, received, and was correctly charged for. The cycle is:

  1. PO issuance — a purchase order is created and approved before the vendor is engaged.
  2. Vendor invoice received — the bill arrives, and is routed to AP.
  3. 3-way match — AP compares the Purchase Order, the Receiving Report (or delivery confirmation), and the Vendor Invoice. All three must agree on quantity, unit price, and total.
  4. Approval — the responsible PM or budget owner signs off.
  5. Payment — released on the scheduled payment run according to terms.

What happens when they don't match: the invoice is held. You do not pay it. You contact the vendor, document the discrepancy, and request a corrected invoice or credit memo. Paying an invoice that fails the match is the single most common way firms lose money through carelessness, not fraud.

callout.html: Real scenario. Global CAD Solutions invoice GCS-2026-087 billed $42.00 per drafted mile, but the master services agreement (MSA) states $38.50 per mile. On 50 miles that is a $178.75 variance. Do you pay it? No. Hold it, raise a dispute, and request a credit memo for the difference. Document everything.

chunks.title: Analyze an offshore drafting invoice — what to check before paying

hotspots.label: Invoice number
hotspots.info: Enter exactly as printed. Duplicates are rejected by the system to prevent double-payment — a wrong digit here can void the match.

hotspots.label: Project code
hotspots.info: Must be tagged to the specific fiber build (e.g. Tennessee Corridor). This tag drives profitability and the COGS allocation per project.

hotspots.label: Unit price per mile
hotspots.info: Compare against the MSA rate. GCS MSA is $38.50/mile — anything higher is a hold-for-discrepancy, never auto-pay.

hotspots.label: Payment terms
hotspots.info: Net-30, Net-45 — this drives which payment run it lands on and how it ages on the AP report.

hotspots.label: Total + remittance
hotspots.info: Recompute quantity × unit price. Confirm it ties to the PO and receiving report before release.

chunks.title: When the liability is recorded — and the payment run
chunks.bodyHtml:
Under accrual accounting, the AP liability is recorded when the invoice is received and the obligation exists, not when you cut the check. This matters because it changes which period's expenses and liabilities the bill lands in.

Recording the liability: Debit the expense (or asset) account, Credit Accounts Payable. The vendor is now owed, and the cost is in the correct period.

The payment run: a batch of approved invoices selected for payment by due date and cash availability. Payment clears the AP balance — it is a separate, later event. Recording the liability and paying the bill are two distinct journal entries.

AP aging report buckets what you owe by how long it has been sitting:

BucketWhat it tells you
0–30 daysOn terms — normal. The bulk of a healthy AP sits here.
31–60 daysApproaching terms limit — review vendor-by-vendor.
61–90 daysLate. Vendor relationships and early-pay discounts are at risk.
90+ daysDelinquent. Possible late fees, supplier credit holds, and a cash-flow red flag the controller will ask about.

callout.html: Early payment discounts — 2/10 Net 30. "2/10 Net 30" means a 2% discount if paid within 10 days, otherwise full amount due in 30. On a $10,000 invoice that is a $200 saving for paying 20 days early — an implied ~36% annual return. Capturing it deliberately is a real cash management decision a controller will ask you to justify.

chunks.title: Record a vendor invoice on receipt (no cash yet)
chunks.scenarioHtml: Global CAD Solutions sends invoice GCS-2026-091 for $9,625 of offshore LLD drafting on the Tennessee Corridor build. Terms are Net-30. No cash has moved — record the liability now in the correct period.

chunks.title: Process the payment, including the wire fee
chunks.scenarioHtml: 28 days later you wire payment of $9,625 to Global CAD for invoice GCS-2026-091. The bank charges a $25 outgoing wire fee in addition. Record the payment clearing the AP and the fee hitting expense.

chunks.title: W2 employees vs. 1099 contractors — and why accounting cares
chunks.bodyHtml:
The US tax system draws a hard line between two ways a person can do work for your company:

W2 Employee1099 Contractor
On US payroll; Skarion withholds income tax, Social Security, and Medicare.Self-employed; Skarion pays a flat invoice and the contractor handles their own taxes.
Creates payroll tax liabilities — employer FICA, FUTA, SUTA.Routed straight through Accounts Payable as a vendor invoice.
Skarion's 10 US Engineering Project Managers.Offshore drafting firms like Global CAD Solutions.

Why accounting cares: a W2 run touches Cash, a Salary Expense account, and multiple payroll tax liability accounts in one compound entry. A 1099 contractor is a single AP entry. Getting that backwards creates IRS exposure for the company. This is the kind of distinction an interviewer probes to see whether you've actually run payroll.

Skarion's payroll economics:

  • US Team (W2): 10 Project Managers, avg $95,000/year ≈ $79,167/month gross.
  • Employer taxes: FICA 7.65% ≈ $6,056/month, FUTA/SUTA ≈ $200/month.
  • Offshore contractors (1099/AP): ≈ $42,000–$60,000/month in drafting invoices.

chunks.title: Record the full US payroll run
chunks.scenarioHtml: Record January's US payroll. Gross wages: $79,167. Employee withholdings: federal income tax $15,000, Social Security $4,908, Medicare $1,148 — total $21,056 withheld. Employer taxes add $6,256 (the company's own expense). Net pay of $58,111 is wired to employees. Record the gross wage expense, the employer tax expense, the cash out, and the liabilities owed to the government.

chunks.title: Accruals, prepaids, and reversals
chunks.bodyHtml:
Three of the most common interview questions live here, and they are all consequences of the accrual principle:

Accruals. Record an expense before the invoice arrives. Skarion's offshore team completes $12,400 of drafting in January; the invoice does not land until February 5th. The work happened in January, so the expense belongs in January. The January 31st entry: Debit COGS — Subcontractor Drafting $12,400 / Credit Accrued Liabilities $12,400.

Reversals. On February 1st you reverse the accrual (Debit Accrued Liabilities / Credit COGS), so that when the real invoice is entered on February 5th the net effect is exactly one expense, in one period — not double-counted.

Prepaids. Cash paid now, expensed later through an amortization schedule. The $36,000 Vetro license paid July 1st is a $3,000/month expense. Each month: Debit Software Expense $3,000 / Credit Prepaid Software $3,000. This is the single most common adjusting entry you will post at month-end.

chunks.title: AP and payroll vocabulary — know these cold

cards.back: Recording an expense in the period it was incurred, before the invoice arrives. Skarion accrues $12,400 of January offshore work invoiced in February so January's books are complete.
Undoing an accrual entry on the first day of the next period, so the real invoice doesn't double-count the expense. Debit Accrued Liabilities / Credit COGS on Feb 1 cancels the Jan 31 accrual.
Cash paid now for a benefit that spans future periods — recorded as an asset and amortized to expense over time. The $36,000 Vetro license is prepaid, amortized $3,000/month.
The systematic allocation of a prepaid asset to expense across benefited periods. Straight-line amortization of the 12-month Vetro license = $3,000/month for 12 months.
Wages earned by employees but not yet paid at period-end. A month-end accrual captures the days worked before payday so the salary expense lands in the correct month.

chunks.intro: Three accrual and prepaid questions that show up almost verbatim in entry-level interviews.
chunks.title: Accruals, prepaids & AP quiz

questions.q: Skarion's offshore team completes $12,400 of drafting in January. The invoice arrives February 3rd. What is the January 31st entry?
options: Wait — only record it when the invoice arrives in February
Debit COGS — Subcontractor Drafting $12,400 / Credit Accrued Liabilities $12,400
questions.explanation: The expense belongs in January because that is when the work was performed. Accrue it: debit the expense, credit an accrued liability. The entry reverses on Feb 1 so the real Feb 3 invoice doesn't double-count it.

questions.q: Skarion paid $36,000 for a 12-month Vetro license on July 1st, recorded as a prepaid. What is the balance of Prepaid Software Licenses on October 31st?
questions.explanation: From July 1 to Oct 31 is 5 months of amortization at $3,000/month = $15,000 expensed. Remaining prepaid = $36,000 − $15,000 = $21,000 (7 months of benefit left).

questions.q: What is the employer's portion of FICA tax as a percentage of gross wages?
options: 0% — FICA is only an employee tax
questions.explanation: Employer FICA is 7.65% — matching the employee's 7.65%. Both pay 6.2% Social Security (up to the wage cap) plus 1.45% Medicare (no cap). Skarion's ~$79,167 monthly gross ≈ $6,056/month employer FICA.
